title: Warnings and diagnostics

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Warnings and diagnostics; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C++ diagnostics report syntax, constraint, instantiation, overload, or tool-detected risk; the first relevant cause and template context matter more than message volume.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An overload failure is read from the rejected call outward, comparing candidate parameter constraints instead of editing every template frame. Your goal is to repair a set of syntax, type and link errors using compiler evidence. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor; Compile and link model. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C++ diagnostics report syntax, constraint, instantiation, overload, or tool-detected risk; the first relevant cause and template context matter more than message volume. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Compilers format template traces differently, warnings are configurable, ill-formed no-diagnostic-required cases exist, and successful compilation does not prove defined behavior. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: The longest template diagnostic line is necessarily the root cause and all later notes describe separate defects.

includes: Read template-aware diagnostics from the earliest actionable cause and keep warnings enabled.
Repair a set of syntax, type and link errors using compiler evidence.
excludes: Compilers format template traces differently, warnings are configurable, ill-formed no-diagnostic-required cases exist, and successful compilation does not prove defined behavior.

outcomes: Repair a set of syntax, type and link errors using compiler evidence.

examples.title: Warnings and diagnostics: defined case
examples.situation: An overload failure is read from the rejected call outward, comparing candidate parameter constraints instead of editing every template frame.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: An overload failure is read from the rejected call outward, comparing candidate parameter constraints instead of editing every template frame.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Locate the primary failed requirement or call, use candidate notes as context, minimize the example, and treat clean diagnostics as bounded evidence.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Locate the primary failed requirement or call, use candidate notes as context, minimize the example, and treat clean diagnostics as bounded evidence.

examples.title: Warnings and diagnostics: boundary case
examples.situation: Strict conversion warnings expose an unintended signed-to-unsigned change even though the compiler could emit code.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Strict conversion warnings expose an unintended signed-to-unsigned change even though the compiler could emit code.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Compilers format template traces differently, warnings are configurable, ill-formed no-diagnostic-required cases exist, and successful compilation does not prove defined behavior.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: The longest template diagnostic line is necessarily the root cause and all later notes describe separate defects.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Repair a set of syntax, type and link errors using compiler evidence.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The longest template diagnostic line is necessarily the root cause and all later notes describe separate defects.
misconceptions.correction: Locate the primary failed requirement or call, use candidate notes as context, minimize the example, and treat clean diagnostics as bounded evidence.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: The longest template diagnostic line is necessarily the root cause and all later notes describe separate defects.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Warnings and diagnostics: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Compilers format template traces differently, warnings are configurable, ill-formed no-diagnostic-required cases exist, and successful compilation does not prove defined behavior.

recap.summary: You should now be able to repair a set of syntax, type and link errors using compiler evidence. Keep this boundary in view: Compilers format template traces differently, warnings are configurable, ill-formed no-diagnostic-required cases exist, and successful compilation does not prove defined behavior.
